articles.title: Write Amplification in Postgres: The 3-4x Tax on Every Insert
articles.summary: You're inserting 100 MB/sec of sensor data into Postgres. Your monitoring dashboard, meanwhile, is cheerfully reporting 300-400 MB/sec of disk writes. You check the math twice. You check it a third time. The math does not improve. Welcome to write amplification. This is the ratio between the logical data you intended to write and the physical I/O Postgres actually performs. articles.title: Building the agentic cloud: everything we launched during Agents Week 2026
articles.summary: Today marks the end of our first Agents Week, an innovation week dedicated entirely to the age of agents. It couldn’t have been more timely: over the past year, agents have swiftly changed how people work. Coding agents are helping developers ship faster than ever. Support agents resolve tickets end-to-end. Research agents validate hypotheses across hundreds of sources in minutes. And people aren'

articles.title: The AI engineering stack we built internally — on the platform we ship

articles.title: Orchestrating AI Code Review at scale
articles.summary: Code review is a fantastic mechanism for catching bugs and sharing knowledge, but it is also one of the most reliable ways to bottleneck an engineering team. A merge request sits in a queue, a reviewer eventually context-switches to read the diff, they leave a handful of nitpicks about variable naming, the author responds, and the cycle repeats. articles.title: Introducing the Agent Readiness score. Is your site agent-ready?

articles.title: Shared Dictionaries: compression that keeps up with the agentic web
articles.summary: Web pages have grown 6-9% heavier every year for the past decade, spurred by the web becoming more framework-driven, interactive, and media-rich. Nothing about that trajectory is changing. What is changing is how often those pages get rebuilt and how many clients request them. Both are skyrocketing because of agents. Shared dictionaries shrink asset transfers from servers to browsers so pages load

articles.title: Unweight: how we compressed an LLM 22% without sacrificing quality

Now we are tackling the next big bottleneck in our inference platform: model weights. Generating a single toke

articles.title: We beat Google’s zero-knowledge proof of quantum cryptanalysis

articles.title: How GitHub uses eBPF to improve deployment safety
articles.summary: Did you know that, at GitHub, we host all of our own source code on github.com ? We do this because we’re our own biggest customer—testing out changes internally before they go to users. However, there’s one downside: If github.com were ever to go down, we wouldn’t be able to access our own source code. This is what you’d call a very simple circular dependency: to deploy GitHub, we needed GitHub.

articles.title: Cloudflare’s AI Platform: an inference layer designed for agents

articles.title: Simplifying MBA obfuscation with CoBRA
articles.summary: Mixed Boolean-Arithmetic (MBA) obfuscation disguises simple operations like x + y behind tangles of arithmetic and bitwise operators. Malware authors and software protectors rely on it because no standard simplification technique covers both domains simultaneously; algebraic simplifiers don’t understand bitwise logic, and Boolean minimizers can’t handle arithmetic. articles.title: Carelessness versus craftsmanship in cryptography
articles.summary: Two popular AES libraries, aes-js and pyaes, “helpfully” provide a default IV in their AES-CTR API, leading to a large number of key/IV reuse bugs. These bugs potentially affect thousands of downstream projects. When we shared one of these bugs with an affected vendor, strongSwan, the maintainer provided a model response for security vendors. articles.title: Building cryptographic agility into Sigstore
articles.summary: Software signatures carry an invisible expiration date. articles.summary: I’m Ben Johnson, and I work on Litestream at Fly.io. Litestream is the missing backup/restore system for SQLite.
